problem_summary: 2018 MCM Problem C (data): four U.S. border states (CA, AZ, TX, NM) want an interstate "energy compact" focused on cleaner/renewable energy. Using the supplied 50-year ProblemCData.xlsx (1960–2009, 605 variables/state), the team had to: (1) build a comparable energy "profile" per state, (2) determine which state currently has the best profile, (3) forecast the profiles to 2025 and 2050 under no-policy-change, and (4) recommend quantitative renewable-energy targets and concrete actions to reach them, delivered partly as a governor's memo.
models_used: Cubic spline interpolation for sparse missing values; curve fitting for indicators missing <25 years; cross-state averaging (highly-correlated borrowing) for indicators that are mostly zero
Min-max normalization to [0,1] plus per-capita division (by state population) to remove population/area scale effects

Three engineered composite indices: Use of Renewable Energy U_r, Use of Clean Energy U_c (renewables+natural gas+nuclear), and Greenhouse Gas Emissions GE (weighted CO2 from fossil consumption using published lb-CO2/MBtu ratios: coal 214.3, gasoline 157.2, natural gas 117.0)
PCA (Principal Component Analysis) to compress 5 correlated per-sector secondary indicators into U_r and U_c (first two PCs giving 0.93 cumulative for clean, 0.98 for renewable)
MAUA (Multi-Attribute Utility Analysis) with a piecewise power utility function u_i(x)=((x-a)/(b-a))^k calibrated so min→0, max→1, median→0.5 (solving for k_i) to spread out compressed distributions
Combination Weighting Approach (geometric mean of AHP and PCA weights, normalized) to weight the three indices — final weights U_r=0.178, U_c=0.433, GE=-0.389; AHP consistency check CR=0.033<0.1
AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) as the subjective-weight half of the combination weighting

ARIMA(p,q,d) time-series forecasting applied per secondary index (e.g. ARIMA(4,4,2) for CA's CA_r), with d chosen by differencing + p-value stationarity test, p/q read from ACF/PACF, validated by AIC/log-likelihood
Shapley Value (cooperative-game) regression model to split the renewable-investment fund fairly across the four states by marginal contribution
key_techniques: Tiered missing-data strategy: matched the imputation method to the severity of missingness (spline for a few gaps, curve-fit for <25yr, cross-state borrowing for mostly-empty) instead of one blanket fill
Domain-driven feature engineering: collapsed 605 raw variables into 13 interpretable indicators organized by 4 sectors (transport/commercial/industrial/residential) x 3 energy themes
Two-level hierarchical scoring: PCA at the secondary-index level, then combination weighting at the top index level — separating 'correlation-driven' from 'importance-driven' weighting
Utility-function reshaping to fix large magnitude gaps after normalization, pinning the median to 0.5 to maximize discrimination where data density is highest
Combining objective (PCA) and subjective (AHP) weights via geometric mean to hedge the weakness of each
Per-index ARIMA forecasting (not a single aggregate forecast) so the composite score is rebuilt from forecasted components, preserving structure
Reframing the funding-allocation sub-question as a cooperative game and solving it with Shapley values — a fairness-justified, not arbitrary, split
Calibrating recommended targets to 110% of the model's own forecast to express 'cooperation premium' rather than inventing numbers
why_it_won: From a judge's view, this paper wins on three axes. (1) Closure of the full problem chain: it answers every sub-question — profile, best state, 2025/2050 forecast, AND quantified targets + a costed action plan ($/state via Shapley) + a governor's memo — nothing is left as hand-waving. (2) Methodological layering that is justified rather than decorative: it explicitly explains WHY each tool is chosen (PCA because sectors are correlated and AHP 'would lose effect'; combination weighting because both correlation and real-world importance matter; ARIMA because it captures year-over-year dynamics unlike naive curve-fitting). That 'why this method here' reasoning is exactly what graders reward. (3) Defensible, reproducible numbers: it shows PCA loadings, AHP consistency ratio (CR=0.033), ARIMA parameters with AIC/log-likelihood, and concrete weights/coefficients in tables, so a reader can audit the pipeline. The data-science discipline (severity-matched imputation, per-capita normalization, validation statistics) signals the rigor expected of a data-type C problem.
section_highlights: Standout structural moves: the Summary sheet front-loads every quantitative result (weights, 2025/2050 scores, the 34%/59% targets, the $208B fund split) so a triage judge sees the whole answer in 30 seconds. A separate governor's Memo restates findings in plain executive language — directly serving the 'inform the states' framing of the prompt. The 'Influential Factors' subsection (geography/climate, population, economy/policy) connects the numbers back to real-world state characteristics, showing the team understands its own results rather than just computing them. A clean model-overview flowchart (Fig 6) makes the multi-stage pipeline legible before the math. Heavy, varied visualization (energy-structure bars, trend lines, ACF/PACF plots, forecast bands) — the team even lists 'splendid presentations through a variety of charts' as a strength. Explicit Assumptions and a Notations table up front, and an honest Strengths/Weaknesses self-evaluation at the end.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-admitted under-use of data: only 13 of 605 variables drive the score, risking loss of signal the judges' real dataset contained
Subjectivity conceded by the authors themselves: the utility-function form and the ARIMA p/q/d choices are hand-picked; the AHP pairwise judgments are also analyst-set

ARIMA order selection looks shaky — text says ARIMA(4,4,2) but reports four phi and four theta params (q=4 not 2), and d=4 differencing on a 50-point series is very aggressive and prone to overfit/overdifferencing
Forecasting each secondary index independently ignores cross-index correlations and can produce incoherent or out-of-range composite trajectories (some plotted overall scores go negative)
A negative weight on GE (-0.389) makes the composite 'overall score' hard to interpret as a single utility and can be gamed
Imputation by borrowing other states' averages can fabricate structure for indicators that are legitimately zero (e.g. no nuclear), which the paper flags but still does
Internal inconsistency in reported numbers: the fund total appears as both $208B (summary/memo) and $343.85B (Table 9), and per-state shares differ — a credibility ding
Targets set at 110% of forecast is a thin justification for a 'cooperation premium' with no mechanism showing cooperation actually yields that 10%
OCR/figure-data in this markdown copy is partly garbled (e.g. Fig 9 all-zeros, future-dated rows), so some quantitative plots can't be fully trusted from the transcript alone
reusable_patterns: Severity-matched imputation ladder: pick interpolation vs. fitting vs. cross-entity borrowing based on HOW MUCH is missing — reusable for any messy-data C problem
Per-capita (or per-unit) normalization to make heterogeneous entities comparable before scoring

Two-stage weighting architecture: PCA/objective weights for correlated sub-features + AHP/subjective weights for top-level importance, fused by geometric-mean combination weighting (with an AHP consistency check) — a strong general recipe for composite-index / ranking problems
Utility-function reshaping (median→0.5 power curve) to restore discrimination after min-max normalization compresses values
Build a composite index, then forecast its COMPONENTS with ARIMA and recompose — keeps the forecast tied to interpretable drivers; validate ARIMA with ACF/PACF + AIC/log-likelihood
Use Shapley values to justify any 'who pays / how to split' allocation sub-question as fair rather than arbitrary
Anchor recommended targets to the model's own forecast (e.g. forecast x multiplier) so policy numbers are traceable to analysis
Deliverable packaging for C problems: front-loaded quantitative Summary + a stakeholder Memo + a model-overview flowchart + explicit assumptions/notations + honest strengths/weaknesses section
A 'real-world influential factors' section that explains WHY your computed ranking makes sense, tying numbers back to domain context
